label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this locker problem. So, there are 100 lockers, all starting closed. Then there are 100 people, each person k toggles every k-th locker. Toggling means if it's open, close it, and if it's closed, open it. Person 1 opens all lockers because they toggle every first locker, which are all closed initially. Then Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker, so lockers 2, 4, 6, etc. But since they started open (from Person 1), Person 2 will close them. Then Person 3 toggles every 3rd locker, and so on up to Person 100. Hmm, the question is how many lockers are open at the end. I need to figure out for each locker how many times it's toggled. Because if it's toggled an even number of times, it will end up closed (since it starts closed), and if it's toggled an odd number of times, it will be open. So, for each locker number, say locker number n, how many times is it toggled? Well, that's the same as the number of divisors n has. Because Person k toggles locker n if and only if k divides n (since every k-th locker means lockers that are multiples of k). For example, locker 6 is toggled by Person 1, 2, 3, and 6. So divisors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, 6. So 4 divisors, which is even, so locker 6 will end up closed. So the key is to find the numbers between 1 and 100 that have an odd number of divisors. Because those lockers will be open. Now, when does a number have an odd number of divisors? Divisors usually come in pairs. For example, 6 has divisors 1 & 6, 2 & 3. Each pair multiplies to 6. However, if the number is a perfect square, like 16, one of the divisors is repeated (4*4=16), so it's only counted once. So perfect squares have an odd number of divisors. Therefore, the lockers that are left open are the ones that are perfect squares. So how many perfect squares are there between 1 and 100 inclusive? Well, the squares of 1, 2, 3,... up to 10, because 10^2 is 100. So that's 10 numbers. Therefore, there should be 10 lockers left open. Wait, let me check with a small example. Let's take 10 lockers instead of 100. Perfect squares would be 1,4,9. So 3 lockers. Let's simulate: Person 1 opens all lockers (1-10 open). Person 2 toggles 2,4,6,8,10 (so closes them; now lockers 1,3,5,7,9 open). Person 3 toggles 3,6,9; locker 3 was open, now closed; 6 was closed, now open; 9 was open, now closed. Now open lockers: 1,5,7,6. Person 4 toggles 4,8; locker 4 was closed, now open; 8 was open, now closed. Open lockers: 1,5,7,4,6. Person 5 toggles 5,10; locker 5 was open, now closed; 10 was closed, now open. Open lockers: 1,7,4,6,10. Person 6 toggles 6; locker 6 was open, now closed. Open lockers: 1,7,4,10. Person 7 toggles 7; locker 7 was open, now closed. Open lockers: 1,4,10. Person 8 toggles 8; locker 8 was closed, now open. But then open lockers: 1,4,10,8. Wait, but according to my earlier logic, it should be perfect squares, which in 1-10 are 1,4,9. But in the simulation with 10 lockers, after 10 people, locker 9 would have been toggled by 1,3,9. So person 9 toggles locker 9. Let me continue the simulation. Person 9 toggles locker 9: locker 9 was closed (toggled by person 3 and 9?), wait let me track: Wait in the simulation above, I stopped at person 7. Let me try again more carefully. But maybe it's easier to just note that in the 10 locker example, the open lockers are 1,4,9. So that's 3, which is
